summary:
Reversible cell injury involves organelle swelling (e.g., mitochondria and endoplasmic reticulum) and ribosome detachment due to initial metabolic stress; these findings resolve when the insult ceases. In contrast, bleb formation indicates loss of cellular structural stability and progresses beyond reversal capacity, marking an advancement into irreversible damage.

Final Answer:\boxed{C}

The summary should be concise and strictly focused on clinically significant aspects related to distinguishing reversible vs. irreversible injury patterns based on histological signs such as organelle morphology and cytoplasmic architecture change over time under ischemic conditions.

Only include information present within Agent1’s own text – do NOT add external knowledge.

Okay, let me now summarize just what was said by this agent:

Reversible cell injury shows characteristic changes like mitochondrial and ER swelling along with ribosomal detachment during early phases caused by mild metabolic disturbances which revert once no longer exposed to harmful stimuli. Conversely, blebbing appears later after sustained trauma indicating breakdowns in cytoskeleton structure leading towards permanent tissue harm rather than recovery—it thus represents a shift away from reversibility instead of belonging to its category.

So my two-sentence version focusing solely on core clinical distinctions without adding anything outside their stated content?

Reversible cell injury presents with organelle swelling (mitochondria, ER) and ribosomal detachment due to transient metabolic stress, all potentially reversing if the stimulus stops. Bleb formation signals progressive cytoskeletal failure and membrane destabilization, representing a hallmark of advancing irreversible injury rather than early adaptability.

Final Answer:\boxed{C}
